infobox.value: * [Association of Commonwealth Universities](/wiki/Association_of_Commonwealth_Universities "Association of Commonwealth Universities") * [European University Association](/wiki/European_University_Association "European University Association") * [Universities UK](/wiki/Universities_UK "Universities UK")

(**UoL**; abbreviated as **Lond** or more rarely **Londin** in [post-nominals](/wiki/Post-nominal_letters "Post-nominal letters")) is a [federal](/wiki/Collegiate_university "Collegiate university")[[a]](#cite_note-11) [public](/wiki/Public_university "Public university") [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") in [London](/wiki/London "London"), England, United Kingdom. The university was established by [royal charter](/wiki/Royal_charter "Royal charter") in 1836 as a degree-awarding [examination board](/wiki/Examination_board "Examination board") for students holding certificates from [University College London](/wiki/University_College_London "University College London"), [King's College London](/wiki/King%27s_College_London "King's College London"), and "other such institutions, corporate or unincorporated, as shall be established for the purpose of Education, whether within the Metropolis or elsewhere within our United Kingdom".[[11]](#cite_note-google-12) It is one of three institutions to have claimed the title of the [third-oldest university in England](/wiki/Third-oldest_university_in_England_debate "Third-oldest university in England debate").[[b]](#cite_note-13)[[12]](#cite_note-harte1986-14)[[13]](#cite_note-England_Old_University-15) It moved to a federal structure with constituent colleges in 1900.[[14]](#cite_note-google2-16) It is now incorporated by its fourth (1863) royal charter and governed by the University of London Act 2018.[[15]](#cite_note-UoL_Governance-17)

The **University of London** (**UoL**; abbreviated as **Lond** or more rarely **Londin** in [post-nominals](/wiki/Post-nominal_letters "Post-nominal letters")) is a [federal](/wiki/Collegiate_university "Collegiate university")[[a]](#cite_note-11) [public](/wiki/Public_university "Public university") [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") in [London](/wiki/London "London"), England, United Kingdom. The university was established by [royal charter](/wiki/Royal_charter "Royal charter") in 1836 as a degree-awarding [examination board](/wiki/Examination_board "Examination board") for students holding certificates from [University College London](/wiki/University_College_London "University College London"), [King's College London](/wiki/King%27s_College_London "King's College London"), and "other such institutions, corporate or unincorporated, as shall be established for the purpose of Education, whether within the Metropolis or elsewhere within our United Kingdom".[[11]](#cite_note-google-12) It is one of three institutions to have claimed the title of the [third-oldest university in England](/wiki/Third-oldest_university_in_England_debate "Third-oldest university in England debate").[[b]](#cite_note-13)[[12]](#cite_note-harte1986-14)[[13]](#cite_note-England_Old_University-15) It moved to a federal structure with constituent colleges in 1900.[[14]](#cite_note-google2-16) It is now incorporated by its fourth (1863) royal charter and governed by the University of London Act 2018.[[15]](#cite_note-UoL_Governance-17)

The university consists of [17 member institutions](/wiki/Member_institutions_of_the_University_of_London "Member institutions of the University of London") and three central academic bodies.[[16]](#cite_note-18)[[17]](#cite_note-19) It has around 48,000 [distance learning external students](/wiki/University_of_London_Worldwide "University of London Worldwide")[[18]](#cite_note-External-20) and around 205,400 [campus-based internal students](/wiki/Member_institutions_of_the_University_of_London "Member institutions of the University of London"), making it the [largest university by number of students](/wiki/List_of_universities_in_the_United_Kingdom_by_enrolment "List of universities in the United Kingdom by enrolment") in the United Kingdom. For most practical purposes, ranging from admissions to funding, the member institutions operate on an independent basis, with many conferring their own degrees whilst remaining in the federal university.

Under the 2018 act, member institutions ceased to be termed colleges and gained the right to seek university status without having to leave the federal university: Birkbeck, City, Goldsmiths, King's College London, London School of Economics and Political Science, London School of Hygiene & Tropical Medicine, Queen Mary, Royal Holloway, Royal Veterinary College, [School of Oriental and African Studies](/wiki/SOAS_University_of_London "SOAS University of London"), St George's, and University College London have all indicated that they intend to do so.[[19]](#cite_note-Member_institutions_seeking_university_status-21)

As of 2015, there are around 2 million [University of London alumni](/wiki/List_of_University_of_London_people "List of University of London people") across the world,[[20]](#cite_note-UOL_Alumni-22) including at least 14 monarchs or royalty, more than 60 presidents or prime ministers (including five [prime ministers of the United Kingdom](/wiki/List_of_Prime_Ministers_of_the_United_Kingdom "List of Prime Ministers of the United Kingdom")),[[c]](#cite_note-23) two [Cabinet Secretaries of the UK](/wiki/Cabinet_Secretary_(United_Kingdom) "Cabinet Secretary (United Kingdom)"),[[d]](#cite_note-24) 98 [Nobel laureates](/wiki/Nobel_Prize "Nobel Prize"),[[e]](#cite_note-25) five [Fields Medallists](/wiki/Fields_Medal "Fields Medal"), four [Turing Award](/wiki/Turing_Award "Turing Award") winners, six [Grammy](/wiki/Grammy_Award "Grammy Award") winners, two [Oscar](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards") winners, three [Olympic gold medalists](/wiki/List_of_multiple_Olympic_gold_medalists "List of multiple Olympic gold medalists") and the "[Father of the Nation](/wiki/Father_of_the_Nation "Father of the Nation")" of several countries.[[f]](#cite_note-26) The university owns the [University of London Press](/wiki/University_of_London_Press "University of London Press").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_London&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_London&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 19th century")]

See also: [London University (UK Parliament constituency)](/wiki/London_University_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "London University (UK Parliament constituency)") and [General Examination for Women](/wiki/General_Examination_for_Women "General Examination for Women")

> All universities are different, but some are more different than others. The University of London is the most different of them all.

— Negley Harte, historian[[21]](#cite_note-UOL_Negley-27)

[University College London](/wiki/University_College_London "University College London") (UCL) was founded under the name "London University" (but without recognition by the state) in 1826 as a secular alternative to the universities of [Oxford](/wiki/University_of_Oxford "University of Oxford") and [Cambridge](/wiki/University_of_Cambridge "University of Cambridge"), which limited their degrees to members of the [established](/wiki/State_religion "State religion") [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England").[[22]](#cite_note-28) As a result of the controversy surrounding UCL's establishment, [King's College London](/wiki/King%27s_College_London "King's College London") was founded as an [Anglican](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") college by royal charter in 1829.[[23]](#cite_note-HIST-29)[[24]](#cite_note-Foundation-30)

In 1830, UCL applied for a royal charter as a university so that it could confer degrees. This was rejected, but renewed in 1834.[[25]](#cite_note-31) In response to this, opposition to "exclusive" rights grew among the London medical schools. The idea of a general degree awarding body for the schools was discussed in the medical press[[26]](#cite_note-32) and in evidence taken by the Select Committee on Medical Education.[[27]](#cite_note-33)[[28]](#cite_note-34) However, the blocking of a bill to open up Oxford and Cambridge degrees to dissenters led to renewed pressure on the Government to grant degree awarding powers to an institution that would not apply religious tests,[[29]](#cite_note-millbanksystems-35)[[30]](#cite_note-millbanksystems2-36)[[31]](#cite_note-jstor-37) particularly as the degrees of the new [University of Durham](/wiki/University_of_Durham "University of Durham") were also to be closed to non-Anglicans.[[32]](#cite_note-millbanksystems3-38)

In 1835, the government announced the response to UCL's petition for a charter. Two charters would be issued, one to UCL incorporating it as a college rather than a university, without degree awarding powers, and a second "establishing a Metropolitan University, with power to grant academical degrees to those who should study at the London University College, or at any similar institution which his Majesty might please hereafter to name".[[33]](#cite_note-millbanksystems4-39)

Following the issuing of its charter on 28 November 1836, the new University of London started drawing up regulations for degrees in March 1837. The death of [William IV](/wiki/William_IV_of_the_United_Kingdom "William IV of the United Kingdom") in June, however, resulted in a problem – the charter had been granted "during our Royal will and pleasure", meaning it was annulled by the king's death.[[34]](#cite_note-google3-40) [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria") issued a second charter on 5 December 1837, reincorporating the university. The university awarded its first degrees in 1839, all to students from UCL and King's College.

The university established by the charters of 1836 and 1837 was essentially an examining board with the right to award degrees in arts, laws and medicine. However, the university did not have the authority to grant degrees in [theology](/wiki/Theology "Theology"), considered the senior faculty in the other three English universities. In medicine, the university was given the right to determine which medical schools provided sufficient medical training. In arts and law, by contrast, it would examine students from UCL, King's College, or any other institution granted a royal warrant, effectively giving the government control of which institutions could submit students for examination by the university. Beyond this right to submit students for examination, there was no other connection between the colleges and the university.

In 1849 the university held its first graduation ceremony at [Somerset House](/wiki/Somerset_House "Somerset House") following a petition to the senate from the graduates, who had previously received their degrees without any ceremony. About 250 students graduated at this ceremony. The [London academic robes](/wiki/Academic_dress_of_the_University_of_London "Academic dress of the University of London") of this period were distinguished by their "rich velvet facings".[[35]](#cite_note-41)

The list of institutions whose students could enter University of London examinations grew rapidly by 1858, including all other British universities as well as more than 30 other schools and colleges outside of London. In that year, a new charter opened up the examinations to everyone, effectively abolishing the weak link between the university and the colleges.[[36]](#cite_note-42)[[37]](#cite_note-43)[[38]](#cite_note-44) This led the [Earl of Kimberley](/wiki/John_Wodehouse,_1st_Earl_of_Kimberley "John Wodehouse, 1st Earl of Kimberley"), a member of the university's senate, to tell the House of Lords in 1888 "that there were no Colleges affiliated to the University of London, though there were some many years ago".[[39]](#cite_note-millbanksystems5-45) The reforms of 1858 also incorporated the graduates of the university into a [convocation](/wiki/Convocation_ceremony "Convocation ceremony"), similar to those of Oxford, Cambridge and Durham, and authorised the granting of degrees in science, the first BSc being awarded in 1860.[[40]](#cite_note-46)

The expanded role meant the university needed more space, particularly with the growing number of students at the provincial [university colleges](/wiki/University_college#United_Kingdom "University college"). Between 1867 and 1870 a new headquarters was built at [6 Burlington Gardens](/wiki/6_Burlington_Gardens "6 Burlington Gardens"), providing the university with exam halls and offices.

In 1863, via a fourth charter, the university gained the right to grant degrees in surgery.[[41]](#cite_note-google5-47) This 1863 charter remains the authority under which the university is incorporated, although all its other provisions were abolished under the University of London Act 1898 ([61 & 62 Vict.](/wiki/61_%26_62_Vict. "61 & 62 Vict.") c. 62).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/Alice_Mary_Marsh_University_of_London_General_Examination_for_Women_certificate_1878.jpg/250px-Alice_Mary_Marsh_University_of_London_General_Examination_for_Women_certificate_1878.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alice_Mary_Marsh_University_of_London_General_Examination_for_Women_certificate_1878.jpg)

[General Examination for Women](/wiki/General_Examination_for_Women "General Examination for Women") certificate from 1878. These were issued 1869–1878, before women were admitted to degrees of the university.

In 1878, the university set another first when it became the first university in the UK to admit women to degrees, via the grant of a supplemental charter. Four female students obtained Bachelor of Arts degrees in 1880 and two obtained Bachelor of Science degrees in 1881, again the first in the country.[[42]](#cite_note-48)

In the late 19th century, the university came under criticism for merely serving as a centre for the administration of tests, and there were calls for a "teaching university" for London. UCL and KCL considered separating from the university to form a separate university, variously known as the Albert University, Gresham University and Westminster University. Following two [royal commissions](/wiki/Royal_commission "Royal commission") the **University of London Act 1898** ([61 & 62 Vict.](/wiki/61_%26_62_Vict. "61 & 62 Vict.") c. 62) was passed, reforming the university and giving it a federal structure with responsibility for monitoring course content and academic standards within its institutions. This was implemented in 1900 with the approval of new statutes for the university.[[43]](#cite_note-UoL_Future-49)

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_London&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 20th century")]

See also: [First Universal Races Congress](/wiki/First_Universal_Races_Congress "First Universal Races Congress")

> The London University should stand to the British empire as the great technological institution in Berlin, the Charlottenburg, stood to the German empire.

— [Lord Rosebery](/wiki/Archibald_Primrose,_5th_Earl_of_Rosebery "Archibald Primrose, 5th Earl of Rosebery") in 1903[[44]](#cite_note-UOL_Rosebery-50)

The reforms initiated by the 1898 act came into force with the approval of the new federal statutes in 1900. Many of the colleges in London became schools of the university, including UCL, King's College, [Bedford College](/wiki/Bedford_College,_London "Bedford College, London"), [Royal Holloway](/wiki/Royal_Holloway,_University_of_London "Royal Holloway, University of London") and the [London School of Economics](/wiki/London_School_of_Economics "London School of Economics"). [Regent's Park College](/wiki/Regent%27s_Park_College "Regent's Park College"), which had affiliated in 1841, became an official divinity school of the university in 1901 (the new statutes having given London the right to award degrees in theology) and [Richmond (Theological) College](/wiki/Richmond_Theological_College "Richmond Theological College") followed as a divinity school of the university in 1902; [Goldsmiths College](/wiki/Goldsmiths_College,_University_of_London "Goldsmiths College, University of London") joined in 1904; [Imperial College](/wiki/Imperial_College_London "Imperial College London") was founded in 1907; [Queen Mary College](/wiki/Queen_Mary,_University_of_London "Queen Mary, University of London") joined in 1915; the [School of Oriental and African Studies](/wiki/School_of_Oriental_and_African_Studies "School of Oriental and African Studies") was founded in 1916; and [Birkbeck College](/wiki/Birkbeck,_University_of_London "Birkbeck, University of London"), which was founded in 1823, joined in 1920.

The previous provision for colleges outside London was not abandoned on federation, instead London offered two routes to degrees: "internal" degrees offered by schools of the university and "external" degrees offered at other colleges (now the [University of London flexible and distance learning programmes](/wiki/University_of_London_(Worldwide) "University of London (Worldwide)")).

UCL and King's College, whose campaign for a teaching university in London had resulted in the university's reconstitution as a federal institution, went even further than becoming schools of the university and were actually merged into it. UCL's merger, under the [University College London (Transfer) Act 1905](/wiki/University_College_London_(Transfer)_Act_1905 "University College London (Transfer) Act 1905") ([5 Edw. 7](/wiki/5_Edw._7 "5 Edw. 7"). c. xci), happened in 1907. The charter of 1836 was surrendered and all of UCL's property became the University of London's. King's College followed in 1910 under the [King's College London (Transfer) Act 1908](/wiki/King%27s_College_London_(Transfer)_Act_1908 "King's College London (Transfer) Act 1908") ([8 Edw. 7](/wiki/8_Edw._7 "8 Edw. 7"). c. xxxix). This was a slightly more complicated case, as the theological department of the college (founded in 1846) did not merge into the university but maintained a separate legal existence under King's College's 1829 charter.[[45]](#cite_note-51)

The expansion of the university's role meant that the Burlington Garden premises were insufficient, and in March 1900 it moved to the Imperial Institute in [South Kensington](/wiki/South_Kensington "South Kensington").[[46]](#cite_note-google6-52) However, its continued rapid expansion meant that it had outgrown its new premises by the 1920s, requiring yet another move. A large parcel of land in [Bloomsbury](/wiki/Bloomsbury "Bloomsbury") near the [British Museum](/wiki/British_Museum "British Museum") was acquired from [the Duke of Bedford](/wiki/Herbrand_Russell,_11th_Duke_of_Bedford "Herbrand Russell, 11th Duke of Bedford") and [Charles Holden](/wiki/Charles_Holden "Charles Holden") was appointed architect with the instruction to create a building "not to suggest a passing fashion inappropriate to buildings which will house an institution of so permanent a character as a University." This unusual remit may have been inspired by the fact that [William Beveridge](/wiki/William_Beveridge "William Beveridge"), having just become director of LSE, upon asking a taxi driver to take him to the University of London was met with the response "Oh, you mean the place near the [Royal School of Needlework](/wiki/Royal_School_of_Needlework "Royal School of Needlework")".[[47]](#cite_note-Hill-53) Holden responded by designing [Senate House](/wiki/Senate_House_(University_of_London) "Senate House (University of London)"), the current headquarters of the university, and at the time of completion the second largest building in London.[[48]](#cite_note-54)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Yeomanry_House%2C_Handel_St%2C_London.jpg/250px-Yeomanry_House%2C_Handel_St%2C_London.jpg)](/wiki/File:Yeomanry_House,_Handel_St,_London.jpg)

Yeomanry House in Handel Street is the home of London UOTC. The flag seen flying is the coat of arms of the University of London.

The University of London contingent of the [Officers' Training Corps](/wiki/Officers%27_Training_Corps "Officers' Training Corps") (OTC) was formed in 1908 and had enrolled 950 students by autumn 1914.[[49]](#cite_note-spiers13-55) During the First World War, the OTC supplied 500 officers to the [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army") between August 1914 and March 1915.[[50]](#cite_note-beckett60-56) Some 665 officers associated with the university died during the First World War[[51]](#cite_note-57) and 245 officers in the Second World War.[[52]](#cite_note-58) As of 2004[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=University_of_London&action=edit) the London University Officers' Training Corps (UOTC), drawn from 52 universities and colleges in the London area (not just the University of London), was the largest UOTC in the country, with about 400 officer cadets.[[53]](#cite_note-59) It has been based at [Yeomanry House](/wiki/Yeomanry_House,_Bloomsbury "Yeomanry House, Bloomsbury") in Handel Street, London since 1992. In 2011, Canterbury Company was founded to recruit officer cadets from universities in Kent.[[54]](#cite_note-60)

During the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"), the colleges of the university (with the exception of Birkbeck) and their students left London for safer parts of the UK, while Senate House was used by the [Ministry of Information](/wiki/Ministry_of_Information_(United_Kingdom) "Ministry of Information (United Kingdom)"), with its roof becoming an observation point for the [Royal Observer Corps](/wiki/Royal_Observer_Corps "Royal Observer Corps"). Though the building was hit by bombs several times, it emerged from the war largely unscathed; rumour at the time had it that the reason the building had fared so well was that [Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler") had planned to use it as his headquarters in London.[[55]](#cite_note-61)

The latter half of the last century was less eventful. In 1948, Athlone Press was founded as the publishing house for the university, and sold to the Bemrose Corporation in 1979,[[56]](#cite_note-AIM25-62) subsequent to which it was acquired by [Continuum publishing](/wiki/Continuum_International_Publishing_Group "Continuum International Publishing Group").[[57]](#cite_note-allbusines.com2006-63) However, the post-WWII period was mostly characterised by expansion and consolidation within the university, such as the acquisition as a constituent body of the Jesuit theological institution Heythrop College on its move from Oxfordshire in 1969.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]`

The **University of London Act 1978** (c. ii) saw the university defined as a federation of self-governing colleges, starting the process of decentralisation that would lead to a marked transference of academic and financial power in this period from the central authorities in Senate House to the individual colleges. In the same period, UCL and King's College regained their legal independence via acts of parliament and the issuing of new royal charters. UCL was reincorporated in 1977, while King's College's new charter in 1980 reunited the main body of the college with the corporation formed in 1829. In 1992 centralised graduation ceremonies at the [Royal Albert Hall](/wiki/Royal_Albert_Hall "Royal Albert Hall") were replaced by individual ceremonies at the colleges.[[58]](#cite_note-64) One of the largest shifts in power of this period came in 1993, when [HEFCE](/wiki/HEFCE "HEFCE") (now the Office for Students, OfS[[59]](#cite_note-65)) switched from funding the University of London, which then allocated money to the colleges, to funding the colleges directly and them paying a contribution to the university.[[43]](#cite_note-UoL_Future-49)

There was also a tendency in the late 20th century for smaller colleges to be amalgamated into larger "super-colleges". Some of the larger colleges (most notably UCL, King's College, LSE and Imperial) periodically put forward the possibility of their departure from the university, although no steps were taken to actually putting this into action until the early 21st century.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_London&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 21st century")]

See also: [London Student](/wiki/London_Student "London Student")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Imperial_Institute.jpg/250px-Imperial_Institute.jpg)](/wiki/File:Imperial_Institute.jpg)

The [Imperial Institute](/wiki/Commonwealth_Institute "Commonwealth Institute") building in [South Kensington](/wiki/South_Kensington "South Kensington"), home to the university from 1900 to 1937

In 2002, [Imperial College](/wiki/Imperial_College "Imperial College") and [UCL](/wiki/University_College_London "University College London") mooted the possibility of a merger, raising the question of the future of the University of London and the smaller colleges within it. Subsequently, considerable opposition from academic staff of both UCL and Imperial led to a rejection of the merger.[[60]](#cite_note-66)

Despite this failure, the trend of decentralising power continued. A significant development in this process was the closing down of the [Convocation](/wiki/Convocation "Convocation") of all the university's alumni in October 2003; this recognised that individual college alumni associations were now increasingly the centre of focus for alumni.[[61]](#cite_note-london-67) However, the university continued to grow even as it moved to a looser federation, and, in 2005, admitted the [Central School of Speech and Drama](/wiki/Central_School_of_Speech_and_Drama "Central School of Speech and Drama").

On 9 December 2005, Imperial College became the second constituent body (after Regent's Park College) to make a formal decision to leave the university. Its council announced that it was beginning negotiations to withdraw from the university in time for its own centenary celebrations, and in order to be able to award its own degrees. On 5 October 2006, the University of London accepted Imperial's formal request to withdraw from it.[[62]](#cite_note-68) Imperial became fully independent on 9 July 2007, as part of the celebrations of the college's centenary.

The *[Times Higher Education Supplement](/wiki/Times_Higher_Education_Supplement "Times Higher Education Supplement")* announced in February 2007 that the London School of Economics, University College London and King's College London all planned to start awarding their own degrees, rather than degrees from the federal University of London as they had done previously, from the start of the academic year starting in Autumn 2007. Although this plan to award their own degrees did not amount to a decision to leave the University of London, the *THES* suggested that this "rais[ed] new doubts about the future of the federal University of London".[[63]](#cite_note-THES-69)

The [School of Pharmacy, University of London](/wiki/School_of_Pharmacy,_University_of_London "School of Pharmacy, University of London"), merged with UCL on 1 January 2012, becoming the UCL School of Pharmacy within the Faculty of Life Sciences.[[64]](#cite_note-70) This was followed on 2 December 2014 by the [Institute of Education](/wiki/Institute_of_Education "Institute of Education") also merging with UCL, becoming the UCL Institute of Education.[[65]](#cite_note-ucl-71)

Since 2010, the university has been outsourcing support services such as cleaning and portering. This has prompted industrial action by the largely [Latin American](/wiki/Latin_American "Latin American") workforce under the "3Cosas" campaign (the 3Cosas – *3 things* – being [sick pay](/wiki/Sick_pay "Sick pay"), [holiday pay](/wiki/Holiday_pay "Holiday pay"), and pensions for outsourced workers on parity with staff employed directly by the university). The 3Cosas campaigners were members of the [UNISON](/wiki/UNISON "UNISON") trade union. However, documents leaked in 2014 revealed that UNISON representatives tried to counter the 3Cosas campaign in meetings with university management.[[66]](#cite_note-72) The 3Cosas workers subsequently transferred to the Independent Workers Union of Great Britain.

Following good results in the [Research Excellence Framework](/wiki/Research_Excellence_Framework "Research Excellence Framework") in December 2014, [City University London](/wiki/City_University_London "City University London") said that they were exploring the possibility of joining the University of London.[[67]](#cite_note-theguardian-73) It was subsequently announced in July 2015 that City would join the University of London in August 2016.[[68]](#cite_note-Grove-74) It will cease to be an independent university and become a college as "City, University of London".[[69]](#cite_note-75)

In 2016 reforms were proposed that would see the colleges become member institutions and be allowed to legally become universities in their own right. A bill to amend the university's statutes was introduced into the House of Lords in late 2016. The bill was held up by procedural matters in the House of Commons, with MP [Christopher Chope](/wiki/Christopher_Chope "Christopher Chope") objecting to it receiving a second [reading](/wiki/Reading_(legislature) "Reading (legislature)") without debate and no time having been scheduled for such debate. Twelve of the colleges, including UCL and King's, said that they would seek university status once the bill was passed.[[70]](#cite_note-76)[[71]](#cite_note-77) The bill was debated and passed its second reading on 16 October 2018.[[72]](#cite_note-78) It received royal assent on 20 December 2018, becoming the University of London Act 2018 (c. iii)[[73]](#cite_note-79) The twelve colleges (namely, all except The Courtauld, ICR, LBS, RAM and RCSSD) subsequently applied for university status, although stating they did not intend to change their names, with notice being given in the [London Gazette](/wiki/London_Gazette "London Gazette") on 4 February 2019.[[74]](#cite_note-80)

In 2018, [Heythrop College](/wiki/Heythrop_College "Heythrop College") became the first major British higher education institution to close since the medieval [University of Northampton](/wiki/University_of_Northampton_(13th_century) "University of Northampton (13th century)") in 1265.[[75]](#cite_note-Heythrop-81) Its library of more than 250,000 volumes was moved to [Senate House Library](/wiki/Senate_House_Library "Senate House Library").[[76]](#cite_note-82)

In 2019, the [University of London Press](/wiki/University_of_London_Press "University of London Press"), founded in 1910, was relaunched as a fully [open-access](/wiki/Open_access "Open access") publisher specializing in "distinctive scholarship at the forefront of the [Humanities](/wiki/Humanities "Humanities")".[[77]](#cite_note-83)

## Campuses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_London&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Campuses")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Senate_House%2C_University_of_London.jpg/250px-Senate_House%2C_University_of_London.jpg)](/wiki/File:Senate_House,_University_of_London.jpg)

[Senate House](/wiki/Senate_House,_London "Senate House, London"), constructed 1932–1937: the headquarters of the University of London

The university owns a considerable [central London](/wiki/Central_London "Central London") estate of 12 hectares of freehold land in [Bloomsbury](/wiki/Bloomsbury "Bloomsbury"), near [Russell Square tube station](/wiki/Russell_Square_tube_station "Russell Square tube station").[[78]](#cite_note-84)

Some of the university's colleges have their main buildings on the estate. The Bloomsbury Campus also contains eight Halls of Residence and [Senate House](/wiki/Senate_House_(University_of_London) "Senate House (University of London)"), which houses [Senate House Library](/wiki/Senate_House_Library "Senate House Library"), the chancellor's official residence and previously housed the [School of Slavonic and East European Studies](/wiki/School_of_Slavonic_and_East_European_Studies "School of Slavonic and East European Studies"), now part of [University College London](/wiki/University_College_London "University College London") (UCL) and housed in its own new building. Almost all of the [School of Advanced Study](/wiki/School_of_Advanced_Study "School of Advanced Study") is housed in Senate House and neighbouring Stewart House.[[79]](#cite_note-85)

The university also owns many of the squares that formed part of the Bedford Estate, including [Gordon Square](/wiki/Gordon_Square "Gordon Square"), [Tavistock Square](/wiki/Tavistock_Square "Tavistock Square"), [Torrington Square](/wiki/Torrington_Square "Torrington Square") and [Woburn Square](/wiki/Woburn_Square "Woburn Square"), as well as several properties outside Bloomsbury, with many of the university's colleges and institutes occupying their own estates across London:

* [Clare Market](/wiki/Clare_Market "Clare Market"),
* The [Aldwych](/wiki/Aldwych "Aldwych"), where the [London School of Economics and Political Science](/wiki/London_School_of_Economics_and_Political_Science "London School of Economics and Political Science") and part of [King's College London](/wiki/King%27s_College_London "King's College London") are based
* The North and East Wings of [Somerset House](/wiki/Somerset_House "Somerset House"), the location for the [Courtauld Institute of Art](/wiki/Courtauld_Institute_of_Art "Courtauld Institute of Art") and [King's College London](/wiki/King%27s_College_London "King's College London"), respectively
* [St Bartholomew's Hospital](/wiki/St_Bartholomew%27s_Hospital "St Bartholomew's Hospital"),
* the [University of London Boat Club](/wiki/University_of_London_Boat_Club "University of London Boat Club") in [Chiswick](/wiki/Chiswick "Chiswick"), and
* The campus of [Royal Holloway and Bedford New College](/wiki/Royal_Holloway_and_Bedford_New_College "Royal Holloway and Bedford New College") including the historic [Founder's Building](/wiki/Founder%27s_Building "Founder's Building").

The university also has several properties outside London, including a number of residential and catering units further afield and the premises of the [University of London Institute in Paris](/wiki/University_of_London_Institute_in_Paris "University of London Institute in Paris"), which offers undergraduate and postgraduate degrees in French and historical studies.

## Organisation and administration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_London&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Organisation and administration")]

See also: [List of Vice-Chancellors of the University of London](/wiki/List_of_Vice-Chancellors_of_the_University_of_London "List of Vice-Chancellors of the University of London")

The university's board of trustees, the governing and executive body of the university, comprises eleven appointed independent persons – all of whom are non-executive; the vice-chancellor, the deputy vice chancellor and four heads of member institutions, appointed by the Collegiate Council.

The board of trustees is supported by the Collegiate Council, which comprises the heads of the member institutions of the university, the deputy vice-chancellor, the dean and chief executive of the School of Advanced Study, the chief executive of the University of London Worldwide and the Collegiate Council's chair, the vice-chancellor.

## Member institutions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_London&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Member institutions")]

Main article: [Member institutions of the University of London](/wiki/Member_institutions_of_the_University_of_London "Member institutions of the University of London")

For most practical purposes, ranging from admission of students to negotiating funding from the government, the 17 member institutions are treated as individual universities. Legally speaking they are known as *Recognised Bodies*, with the authority to examine students and award them degrees of the university. Some member institutions also have the power to award their own degrees instead of those of the university.

Most decisions affecting the member institutions and institutes of the University of London are made at the level of the member institutions or institutes themselves. The University of London does retain its own decision-making structure, however, with the Collegiate Council and board of trustees, responsible for matters of academic policy. The Collegiate Council is made up of the heads of member institutions of the university.[[10]](#cite_note-Collegiate_Council-10)

The 12 institutes, or *Listed Bodies*, within the University of London offer courses leading to degrees that are both examined and awarded by the University of London. Additionally, twelve universities in England, several in Canada and many in other [Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") countries (notably in East Africa) began life as associate colleges of the university offering such degrees. By the 1970s, almost all of these colleges had achieved independence from the University of London. An increasing number of overseas and UK-based academic institutes offer courses to support students registered for the [University of London](/wiki/University_of_London_International_Programmes "University of London International Programmes") flexible and distance learning diplomas and degrees and the Teaching Institutions Recognition Framework enables the recognition of these institutions.

### Colleges in special relation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_London&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Colleges in special relation")]

Main article: [University of London International Programmes](/wiki/University_of_London_International_Programmes "University of London International Programmes")

Between 1946 and 1970, the university entered into 'schemes of special relation' with university colleges in the [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations"). These schemes encouraged the development of independent universities by offering a relationship with the University of London. University colleges in these countries were granted a [royal charter](/wiki/Royal_charter "Royal charter"). An academic board of the university college negotiated with the University of London over the entrance requirements for the admission of students, syllabuses, examination procedures and other academic matters. During the period of the special relationship, graduates of the colleges were awarded University of London degrees.

Some of the colleges which were in special relation are listed below, along with the year in which their special relation was established.

* 1946 – The University College of the West Indies, until 1961 (now the [University of the West Indies](/wiki/University_of_the_West_Indies "University of the West Indies"))[[100]](#cite_note-106)
* 1948 – University College of the Gold Coast (now [University of Ghana](/wiki/University_of_Ghana "University of Ghana"))
* 1948 – University College, Ibadan, until 1967 (now the [University of Ibadan](/wiki/University_of_Ibadan "University of Ibadan"))[[101]](#cite_note-107)
* 1956 – University College of Rhodesia and Nyasaland (now the [University of Zimbabwe](/wiki/University_of_Zimbabwe "University of Zimbabwe")).
* 1961 – Royal College Nairobi (now the [University of Nairobi](/wiki/University_of_Nairobi "University of Nairobi")).
* 1963 – [University of East Africa](/wiki/University_of_East_Africa "University of East Africa") (In 1970, it was split into three independent universities, which are now: [University of Nairobi](/wiki/University_of_Nairobi "University of Nairobi"), [Makerere University](/wiki/Makerere_University "Makerere University"), and [University of Dar es Salaam](/wiki/University_of_Dar_es_Salaam "University of Dar es Salaam"))

In 1970, the 'Schemes of Special Relation' were phased out.

## Coat of arms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_London&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Coat of arms")]

The University of London received a grant of arms in April 1838.[[12]](#cite_note-harte1986-14) The arms depict a [cross of St George](/wiki/Saint_George%27s_Cross "Saint George's Cross") upon which there is a [Tudor rose](/wiki/Tudor_rose "Tudor rose") surrounded by detailing and surmounted by a crown. Above all of this there is a blue field with an open book upon it.

The arms are described in the grant as:

Coat of arms of the University of London

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | Granted  1838  Escutcheon  Argent, the Cross of St George, thereon the Union Rose irradiated and ensigned with the Imperial Crown proper, a Chief Azure, thereon an open Book also proper, Clasps gold[[12]](#cite_note-harte1986-14) |

## Academic dress

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_London&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Academic dress")]

Main article: [Academic dress of the University of London](/wiki/Academic_dress_of_the_University_of_London "Academic dress of the University of London")

The University of London had established a rudimentary code for [academic dress](/wiki/Academic_dress "Academic dress") by 1844. The university was the first to devise a system of academic dress based on faculty colours, an innovation that was subsequently followed by many other universities.

Colleges that award their own degrees have their own academic dress for those degrees.

## Student life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_London&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Student life")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/University_of_London_Union%2C_Malet_Street%2C_London-22April2008.jpg/250px-University_of_London_Union%2C_Malet_Street%2C_London-22April2008.jpg)](/wiki/File:University_of_London_Union,_Malet_Street,_London-22April2008.jpg)

The main building of the [University of London Union](/wiki/University_of_London_Union "University of London Union") (rebranded as 'Student Central, London', then repurposed after 2022 as an expansion of the Birkbeck campus.)

In 2024/25, approximately 5% of all UK students attended one of the University of London's [affiliated schools](/wiki/Federated_school "Federated school").[[5]](#cite_note-HESA_citation-5) Additionally, more than 50,000 students are part of [University of London Worldwide](/wiki/University_of_London_Worldwide "University of London Worldwide").[[18]](#cite_note-External-20)

The ULU building on [Malet Street](/wiki/Malet_Street "Malet Street") (close to Senate House) was home to the [University of London Union](/wiki/University_of_London_Union "University of London Union"), which acted as the [student union](/wiki/Students%27_union "Students' union") for all University of London students alongside the individual college and institution unions. The building was briefly rebranded as "Student Central, London" from 2010 to 2022, offering full membership to current University of London students, and associate membership to students at other universities, and other groups. The union previously owned *[London Student](/wiki/London_Student "London Student"),* the largest student newspaper in Europe, which now runs as a digital news organisation[[102]](#cite_note-108)[[103]](#cite_note-109) However, since 2022, the ULU building was acquired by Birkbeck and converted into an extension of its campus, meaning the UoL no longer has a formal space for its federal student members.[[104]](#cite_note-110)

### Sports, clubs and traditions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_London&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Sports, clubs and traditions")]

Though most sports teams are organised at the college level, [ULU](/wiki/ULU "ULU") ran several sports clubs of its own, some of which (for example the rowing team) compete in [BUCS](/wiki/British_Universities_%26_Colleges_Sport "British Universities & Colleges Sport") leagues. The union also organised leagues for college teams to participate in. These leagues and sports clubs are supported by Friends of University of London Sport which aims to promote them.

In addition to these, ULU catered for sports not covered by the individual colleges through clubs such as the [University of London Union Lifesaving Club](/w/index.php?title=University_of_London_Union_Lifesaving_Club&action=edit&redlink=1 "University of London Union Lifesaving Club (page does not exist)"), which helps students gain awards and learn new skills in [lifesaving](/wiki/Lifesaving "Lifesaving") as well as sending teams to compete throughout the country in the [BULSCA](/wiki/BULSCA "BULSCA") league.

ULU also organised several societies, ranging from Ballroom and Latin American Dance to [Shaolin Kung Fu](/wiki/Shaolin_Kung_Fu "Shaolin Kung Fu"), and from the [University of London Big Band](/w/index.php?title=University_of_London_Big_Band&action=edit&redlink=1 "University of London Big Band (page does not exist)") to the [Breakdancing](/wiki/Breakdancing "Breakdancing") Society. Affiliated to the university is the [University of London Society of Change Ringers](/wiki/University_of_London_Society_of_Change_Ringers "University of London Society of Change Ringers"), a society for bellringers at all London universities.

The university runs the [University of London Boat Club](/wiki/University_of_London_Boat_Club "University of London Boat Club").

A large number of famous individuals have passed through the University of London, either as staff or students, including at least 12 monarchs or royalty, 52 presidents or prime ministers, 84 [Nobel laureates](/wiki/Nobel_Prize "Nobel Prize"), 6 [Grammy](/wiki/Grammy_Award "Grammy Award") winners, 2 [Oscar](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards") winners, 1 [Ekushey Padak](/wiki/Ekushey_Padak "Ekushey Padak") winner and 3 [Olympic gold medalists](/wiki/List_of_multiple_Olympic_gold_medalists "List of multiple Olympic gold medalists"). The [collegiate](/wiki/Collegiate_university "Collegiate university") [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") has also produced [Father of the Nation](/wiki/Father_of_the_Nation "Father of the Nation") for several countries, including several members of [Colonial Service](/wiki/Colonial_Service "Colonial Service") and [Imperial Civil Service](/wiki/Indian_Civil_Service_(British_India) "Indian Civil Service (British India)") during the [British Raj](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") and the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire").

Staff and students of the university, past and present, have contributed to a number of important scientific advances, including the discovery of [vaccines](/wiki/Vaccine "Vaccine") by [Edward Jenner](/wiki/Edward_Jenner "Edward Jenner") and [Henry Gray](/wiki/Henry_Gray "Henry Gray") (author of *[Gray's Anatomy](/wiki/Gray%27s_Anatomy "Gray's Anatomy")*). Additional vital progress was made by University of London people in the following fields: the discovery of the structure of [DNA](/wiki/DNA "DNA") ([Francis Crick](/wiki/Francis_Crick "Francis Crick"), [Maurice Wilkins](/wiki/Maurice_Wilkins "Maurice Wilkins") and [Rosalind Franklin](/wiki/Rosalind_Franklin "Rosalind Franklin")); the invention of modern electronic computers ([Tommy Flowers](/wiki/Tommy_Flowers "Tommy Flowers")); the discovery of [penicillin](/wiki/Penicillin "Penicillin") ([Alexander Fleming](/wiki/Alexander_Fleming "Alexander Fleming") and [Ernest Chain](/wiki/Ernest_Chain "Ernest Chain")); the development of [X-ray](/wiki/X-ray "X-ray") technology ([William Henry Bragg](/wiki/William_Henry_Bragg "William Henry Bragg") and [Charles Glover Barkla](/wiki/Charles_Glover_Barkla "Charles Glover Barkla")); discoveries on the mechanism of action of [Interleukin 10](/wiki/Interleukin_10 "Interleukin 10") ([Anne O'Garra](/wiki/Anne_O%27Garra "Anne O'Garra")); the formulation of the theory of [electromagnetism](/wiki/Electromagnetism "Electromagnetism") ([James Clerk Maxwell](/wiki/James_Clerk_Maxwell "James Clerk Maxwell")); the determination of the [speed of light](/wiki/Speed_of_light "Speed of light") ([Louis Essen](/wiki/Louis_Essen "Louis Essen")); the development of [antiseptics](/wiki/Antiseptics "Antiseptics") ([Joseph Lister](/wiki/Joseph_Lister,_1st_Baron_Lister "Joseph Lister, 1st Baron Lister")); the development of [fibre optics](/wiki/Fibre_optics "Fibre optics") ([Charles K. Kao](/wiki/Charles_K._Kao "Charles K. Kao")); and the invention of the telephone ([Alexander Graham Bell](/wiki/Alexander_Graham_Bell "Alexander Graham Bell")).

Notable political figures who have passed through the university include [Billy Strachan](/wiki/Billy_Strachan "Billy Strachan"), [Muhammad Haji Ibrahim Egal](/wiki/Muhammad_Haji_Ibrahim_Egal "Muhammad Haji Ibrahim Egal"), [Romano Prodi](/wiki/Romano_Prodi "Romano Prodi"), [Junichiro Koizumi](/wiki/Junichiro_Koizumi "Junichiro Koizumi"), [Aung San Suu Kyi](/wiki/Aung_San_Suu_Kyi "Aung San Suu Kyi"), [Ramsay MacDonald](/wiki/Ramsay_MacDonald "Ramsay MacDonald"), [Desmond Tutu](/wiki/Desmond_Tutu "Desmond Tutu"), [Basdeo Panday](/wiki/Basdeo_Panday "Basdeo Panday"), [Taro Aso](/wiki/Taro_Aso "Taro Aso"), [Walter Rodney](/wiki/Walter_Rodney "Walter Rodney"), [Nelson Mandela](/wiki/Nelson_Mandela "Nelson Mandela"), [B. R. Ambedkar](/wiki/B._R._Ambedkar "B. R. Ambedkar") and [Mahatma Gandhi](/wiki/Mahatma_Gandhi "Mahatma Gandhi"). [35th President of the United States](/wiki/List_of_Presidents_of_the_United_States "List of Presidents of the United States") [John F. Kennedy](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy "John F. Kennedy") filed an application and paid fees[[116]](#cite_note-JFK_UOL_LSE-126) for a year's study at the LSE, but later fell ill and left the university without taking a single class.[[116]](#cite_note-JFK_UOL_LSE-126)

In the arts, culture and literature the university has produced many notable figures. Writers include novelists [Malcolm Bradbury](/wiki/Malcolm_Bradbury "Malcolm Bradbury"), [G. K. Chesterton](/wiki/G._K._Chesterton "G. K. Chesterton"), [H. G. Wells](/wiki/H._G._Wells "H. G. Wells"), [Thomas Hardy](/wiki/Thomas_Hardy "Thomas Hardy"), [Arthur C. Clarke](/wiki/Arthur_C._Clarke "Arthur C. Clarke") and [J. G. Ballard](/wiki/J._G._Ballard "J. G. Ballard"). [Futurologist](/wiki/Futurologist "Futurologist") [Donald Prell](/wiki/Donald_Prell "Donald Prell"). Artists associated with the university include [Jonathan Myles-Lea](/wiki/Jonathan_Myles-Lea "Jonathan Myles-Lea"), and several of the leading figures in the [Young British Artists](/wiki/Young_British_Artists "Young British Artists") movement (including [Ian Davenport](/wiki/Ian_Davenport_(artist) "Ian Davenport (artist)"), [Tracey Emin](/wiki/Tracey_Emin "Tracey Emin") and [Damien Hirst](/wiki/Damien_Hirst "Damien Hirst")). Outstanding musicians across a wide range include the conductor [Sir Simon Rattle](/wiki/Simon_Rattle "Simon Rattle"), the soprano [Felicity Lott](/wiki/Felicity_Lott "Felicity Lott") and both members of [Gilbert and Sullivan](/wiki/Gilbert_and_Sullivan "Gilbert and Sullivan"), to [Mick Jagger](/wiki/Mick_Jagger "Mick Jagger"), [Elton John](/wiki/Elton_John "Elton John"), [Dido](/wiki/Dido_(singer) "Dido (singer)"), Pakistani singer [Nazia Hassan](/wiki/Nazia_Hassan "Nazia Hassan") (known in South Asia as the "[Queen of Pop](/wiki/Queen_of_Pop "Queen of Pop")"), and Hong Kong singer [Karen Mok](/wiki/Karen_Mok "Karen Mok"), composer [Florence Margaret Spencer Palmer](/wiki/Florence_Margaret_Spencer_Palmer "Florence Margaret Spencer Palmer"), and members of the bands [Coldplay](/wiki/Coldplay "Coldplay"), [Keane](/wiki/Keane_(band) "Keane (band)"), [Suede](/wiki/Suede_(band) "Suede (band)"), [the Velvet Underground](/wiki/The_Velvet_Underground "The Velvet Underground"), [Blur](/wiki/Blur_(band) "Blur (band)"), [Iron Maiden](/wiki/Iron_Maiden "Iron Maiden"), [Placebo](/wiki/Placebo_(band) "Placebo (band)"), [the Libertines](/wiki/The_Libertines "The Libertines"), and [Queen](/wiki/Queen_(band) "Queen (band)").

The university has also played host to film directors ([Christopher Nolan](/wiki/Christopher_Nolan "Christopher Nolan"), [Derek Jarman](/wiki/Derek_Jarman "Derek Jarman")), philosophers ([Karl Popper](/wiki/Karl_Popper "Karl Popper"), [Roger Scruton](/wiki/Roger_Scruton "Roger Scruton")), explorers ([David Livingstone](/wiki/David_Livingstone "David Livingstone")), international academics ([Sam Karunaratne](/wiki/Sam_Karunaratne "Sam Karunaratne")), Riccarton High School Head of Commerce, Tom Neumann and leading businessmen ([Michael Cowpland](/wiki/Michael_Cowpland "Michael Cowpland"), [George Soros](/wiki/George_Soros "George Soros")).

## Controversy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_London&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Controversy")]

In recent years the University of London has seen much controversy surrounding its treatment of staff and students.

In 2012, outsourced cleaning staff ran the "3 Cosas" campaign, fighting for improvements in three areas – sick pay, holiday and pensions. After over a year of high-profile strikes, protests and occupations, concessions were made by the university in terms of sick pay and holidays; however, these improvements were nowhere near to the extent of what was being demanded by the campaign.[[126]](#cite_note-139)

In 2013, after a student occupation in favour of ten demands, including fair pay for workers, a halt to privatisation of the university and an end to plans to shut down the university's student union [ULU](/wiki/ULU "ULU"), police were called, resulting in the violent eviction and arrests of more than 60 students, as well as police violence towards students outside supporting the occupation.[[127]](#cite_note-140) After these events, a high-profile "Cops Off Campus" demonstration was held against the university's security policies, with thousands in attendance.[[128]](#cite_note-141)

In 2018, an article was published by [Vice](/wiki/Vice_Media "Vice Media") that reported on concerns over the university's security arrangements at Senate House, where more than 25 extra private security staff had been brought in. Students who had been involved in an occupation of Senate House were barred from using university facilities, and there were numerous allegations of students being verbally, physically and sexually assaulted by the temporary security staff.[[129]](#cite_note-142)

In December 2018, the [Independent Workers' Union of Great Britain](/wiki/Independent_Workers%27_Union_of_Great_Britain "Independent Workers' Union of Great Britain") called for a boycott of events at the university's central administration buildings, including Senate House, with the aim of putting pressure on the University of London to bring outsourced cleaning, catering and security staff in-house by targeting a revenue stream worth around £40 million per year.[[130]](#cite_note-143)[[131]](#cite_note-144)[[132]](#cite_note-145)

In May 2019, the congress of the [University and College Union](/wiki/University_and_College_Union "University and College Union") voted to boycott the University of London's central administration buildings including Senate House, raising the pressure on the University of London.[[133]](#cite_note-146) Dion Georgiou, an academic supporting the boycott and a member of UCU, wrote a comment piece for *The Guardian* shortly before the vote, urging the congress to approve the motion and claiming that "[outsourced workers] face an intransigent university management, whose response has frequently blended short-termism with heavy-handedness".[[134]](#cite_note-147) The motion was passed two days later.

In May 2025, the incumbent Vice Chancellor, Prof. [Wendy Thomson](/wiki/Wendy_Thomson "Wendy Thomson"), was suspended pending further inquiries on allegations of bullying and poor leadership.[[135]](#cite_note-148) On May 27, 2025, Prof. [David Latchman](/wiki/David_Latchman "David Latchman") assumed the position in an acting capacity.[[136]](#cite_note-149)

## The federal model elsewhere

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_London&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: The federal model elsewhere")]

In 1850, [Queen's University of Ireland](/wiki/Queen%27s_University_of_Ireland "Queen's University of Ireland")[[12]](#cite_note-harte1986-14) was created as a federal university to provide degrees for students from the colleges established at Belfast, Cork and Galway. This was succeeded in 1879 by the [Royal University of Ireland](/wiki/Royal_University_of_Ireland "Royal University of Ireland"), an examining university along the model of the University of London, which was in turn succeeded by the federal [National University of Ireland](/wiki/National_University_of_Ireland "National University of Ireland") in 1908. When the [University of New Zealand](/wiki/University_of_New_Zealand "University of New Zealand") was constituted in 1874,[[137]](#cite_note-University_Global-150) it was a federal university modelled on the University of London, functioning principally as an examining body,[[137]](#cite_note-University_Global-150) as was the [University of the Cape of Good Hope](/wiki/University_of_the_Cape_of_Good_Hope "University of the Cape of Good Hope") when it was constituted in 1875 and authorised to be responsible for examinations throughout [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa").[[137]](#cite_note-University_Global-150) In [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), similar structures were adopted, but on a regional basis.[[137]](#cite_note-University_Global-150) The [University of Toronto](/wiki/University_of_Toronto "University of Toronto") acted as an examining and degree awarding body for the province of [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario") from 1853 to 1887, by utilising an operating model based on that of University of London.[[137]](#cite_note-University_Global-150)

In [India](/wiki/India "India"), to satisfy the urge for higher education and learning,[[138]](#cite_note-UOL_South_Asia-151) three universities were set up at three presidency towns in 1857 on the model of University of London[[138]](#cite_note-UOL_South_Asia-151) as affiliating universities, viz., [University of Calcutta](/wiki/University_of_Calcutta "University of Calcutta"), [University of Mumbai](/wiki/University_of_Mumbai "University of Mumbai") and [University of Madras](/wiki/University_of_Madras "University of Madras").[[138]](#cite_note-UOL_South_Asia-151)[[139]](#cite_note-UOL_South_Asia2-152)

The [University of Wales](/wiki/University_of_Wales "University of Wales") was established in 1893 on a federal model incorporating (originally) colleges in Aberystwyth, Bangor and Cardiff.[[140]](#cite_note-Collegial_Higher_Education-153) A decision to dissolve the University of Wales was made in 2017.[[141]](#cite_note-154)

## Literature and popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_London&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Literature and popular culture")]

See also: [Category:University of London in fiction](/wiki/Category:University_of_London_in_fiction "Category:University of London in fiction")

### Literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_London&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Literature")]

[Dr. Watson](/wiki/Dr._Watson "Dr. Watson"), a fictional character in the [Sherlock Holmes](/wiki/Sherlock_Holmes "Sherlock Holmes") stories by [Sir Arthur Conan Doyle](/wiki/Arthur_Conan_Doyle "Arthur Conan Doyle"), studied [[142]](#cite_note-WP_Life-155)[[143]](#cite_note-MX_SH-156)[[144]](#cite_note-Mystery_Millions-157) at [St Bart's Hospital Medical School](/wiki/Barts_and_The_London_School_of_Medicine_and_Dentistry "Barts and The London School of Medicine and Dentistry") (now part of QMUL), receiving his medical degree from the University London, and met Sherlock Holmes in the chemical laboratory there.[[142]](#cite_note-WP_Life-155)[[145]](#cite_note-London_Holmes-158) [Jim Hacker](/wiki/Jim_Hacker "Jim Hacker"), a fictional character in the 1980s [British sitcom](/wiki/British_sitcom "British sitcom") *[Yes Minister](/wiki/Yes_Minister "Yes Minister")* and its sequel *[Yes, Prime Minister](/wiki/Yes,_Prime_Minister "Yes, Prime Minister")*, received his degree, a third, from the university (LSE).[[146]](#cite_note-UOL_LSE_Films-159)

The use of [Senate House](/wiki/Senate_House,_London "Senate House, London") by the [Ministry of Information](/wiki/Ministry_of_Information_(United_Kingdom) "Ministry of Information (United Kingdom)") during the Second World War inspired books two noted English writers: [Graham Greene](/wiki/Graham_Greene "Graham Greene") set his novel *[The Ministry of Fear](/wiki/The_Ministry_of_Fear "The Ministry of Fear")* (1943) and its film adaptation *[Ministry of Fear](/wiki/Ministry_of_Fear "Ministry of Fear")* by [Fritz Lang](/wiki/Fritz_Lang "Fritz Lang") (1944) in Bloomsbury.[[147]](#cite_note-160) [George Orwell](/wiki/George_Orwell "George Orwell")'s wife Eileen worked in Senate House for the Censorship Department of the Ministry of Information,[[47]](#cite_note-Hill-53) and her experiences inspired the description of the [Ministry of Truth](/wiki/Ministry_of_Truth "Ministry of Truth") in Orwell's 1949 novel *[Nineteen Eighty-Four](/wiki/Nineteen_Eighty-Four "Nineteen Eighty-Four")*.

### Films and others

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_London&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Films and others")]

A lecturer at the university (SOAS) named [William McGovern](/wiki/William_Montgomery_McGovern "William Montgomery McGovern") was one of the [real-life inspirations](/wiki/Indiana_Jones_(character)#Historical_models "Indiana Jones (character)") of the film character [Indiana Jones](/wiki/Indiana_Jones_(character) "Indiana Jones (character)").[[148]](#cite_note-SOAS_Indiana_Jones-161)

Senate House and the constituent colleges of the University of London have been featured in Hollywood and British films.[[149]](#cite_note-films-162)[[150]](#cite_note-film_london-163)[[151]](#cite_note-164)[[152]](#cite_note-british-film-locations-165)

In 1916, [Alfred Hitchcock](/wiki/Alfred_Hitchcock "Alfred Hitchcock") enrolled at the University of London and took evening courses and drawing and design classes, which later in 1920 helped land him a spot designing title cards.[[153]](#cite_note-Gene_Hitchcock-166)[[154]](#cite_note-Mark_Hitchcock-167)[[155]](#cite_note-Charlotte_Hitchcock-168)[[156]](#cite_note-10_films_Alfred-169)
